ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for ban mian or chilli ban mian; mapped to the broader 'noodle dish' class as the closest defensible ancestor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP (noodle-based dishes) used as the closest defensible category; no specific ban mian entry exists in FoodEx2 as of 2024.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for ban mian or chilli ban mian as of 2024.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical hawker serving of chilli ban mian is approximately 400 g, comprising noodles, broth, minced pork, anchovies, mushrooms, and vegetables in a single bowl.

nutrient_source.title: Chilli Ban Mian — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Chilli Ban Mian (cr_id F05060032). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this record with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for chilli ban mian. GI estimated at 52 based on the closest analogous food: fresh wheat noodles (GI ~52, University of Sydney GI Database). Ban mian uses hand-made flat wheat noodles with a similar starch structure to fresh Chinese wheat noodles. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (52) × carbohydrate per serving (81g for 400g serving) / 100 = 42.1, rounded to 42.0.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 424 mg/100g; estimated 1696 mg per 400 g serving, exceeding 70% of the 2400 mg daily limit in a single meal
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should limit chilli ban mian to small portions, request reduced soy sauce and chilli sauce, ask for anchovies on the side, and increase vegetable content to partially offset sodium with potassium.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate (20.1 g/100g) with low sugar (1.6 g/100g); estimated GI ~52 (low-medium boundary); protein 7.6 g/100g slows gastric emptying
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should control portion size to limit carbohydrate load, avoid additional sweet sauces, and request extra vegetables to increase dietary fibre and further moderate postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium 424 mg/100g is elevated; anchovies provide omega-3 fatty acids which are cardioprotective; saturated fat is low (~1.5 g/100g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should be cautious of the high sodium content per serving and request reduced sauce; the omega-3 contribution from anchovies is beneficial, but overall sodium intake must be managed to avoid adverse blood pressure effects.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anchovies provide anti-inflammatory omega-3 fatty acids; chilli contains nightshade alkaloids (solanine) which may aggravate symptoms in sensitive individuals; sodium 424 mg/100g may worsen tissue swelling
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis may benefit from the omega-3 content of anchovies but should request reduced chilli and sauce to lower sodium and minimise nightshade exposure if sensitivity is known.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chilli Ban Mian (辣味板面) is a popular Singaporean Chinese hawker noodle dish consisting of hand-made flat wheat noodles served in a spicy, flavourful broth. The dish typically includes minced pork, dried anchovies (ikan bilis), mushrooms, leafy green vegetables, and a poached or soft-boiled egg, finished with a chilli paste or sambal for heat. The noodles are hand-pulled or hand-cut to order, giving them a slightly chewy, irregular texture that distinguishes ban mian from machine-extruded noodles. The broth is usually made from pork bones and anchovies, producing a savoury umami base. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides 165 kcal, 7.6 g protein, 6.0 g fat, and 20.1 g carbohydrate, with 2.1 g dietary fibre and 424 mg sodium. The sodium content is elevated and is the primary clinical concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease patients. The moderate carbohydrate level and low sugar content make it manageable for Type 2 Diabetes with portion control. Anchovies contribute omega-3 fatty acids with anti-inflammatory properties relevant to rheumatoid arthritis management. Ban mian is a staple hawker centre meal in Singapore, commonly eaten for lunch or dinner, and is widely available across food courts and hawker stalls island-wide.
common_user_queries: How many calories in chilli ban mian?
Is ban mian suitable for diabetics?
Can I eat ban mian if I have high blood pressure?
What is ban mian made of?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F05060032) with confidence 0.95. The food is a canonical Singaporean hawker dish with an exact HPB record. GI is estimated from the closest analogous food (fresh wheat noodles, University of Sydney GI Database) and is clearly documented as an analogous estimate. Saturated fat is estimated at ~1.5 g/100g based on the known fat composition of minced pork and anchovy broth; this is the only value not directly from HPB.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: detailed micronutrient and fatty acid breakdown for this specific chilli ban mian preparation is not available in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. 2. saturated_fat_g (1.5 g/100g) is estimated based on the typical fat profile of minced pork and anchovy broth; not directly reported by HPB for this record. 3. GI of 52 is an analogous estimate based on fresh Chinese wheat noodles (University of Sydney GI Database); no direct GI measurement for chilli ban mian exists in approved sources. 4. The input record incorrectly classified this dish under primary_cuisine 'western' and dietary_tags 'vegetarian' and 'plant_based'; these have been corrected to 'Singaporean Chinese' and non-vegetarian/non-vegan respectively, as the dish contains minced pork and anchovies. 5. Halal status is marked false as the dish contains pork.
